summary: Joel describes his strategy of exploiting dot-com era companies that burn through venture capital by offering below-cost goods. He recounts buying Mach 3 razor blades from Drugstore.com's $10 promotion and then stocking up from UrbanFetch's going-out-of-business sale. The short post is a sardonic commentary on the absurdity of dot-com business models that essentially gave away investor money to acquire customers. Joel frames razor blades as the real currency of the internet economy.
key_points: Dot-com companies funded by VCs were essentially bribing customers with below-cost goods to create the illusion of a viable business
A savvy consumer strategy during the dot-com bubble was to convert VC subsidies into durable, high-value commodities like razor blades
UrbanFetch going out of business and dumping inventory was a predictable outcome of unsustainable VC-funded retail
The entire dot-com retail model amounted to wealth transfer from venture capitalists to opportunistic consumers
money_quotes: Every once in a while some venture capitalist feels a need to give you some money.
Usually it's because he has a nasty business idea and he needs to bribe people to play along for a while.
The thing about Mach 3 razor blades is that they're quite expensive, and never on sale.
But when you find a stupid venture capitalist trying to give you stuff, if you can get him to give you Mach 3 razor blades, that's basically as good as cash.
Razor blades. The currency of the New Economy.
key_insight: The dot-com bubble's VC-funded customer acquisition was really just a wealth transfer from investors to savvy consumers who treated subsidized goods as free money.
